items.question: What is CART and how does it grow a decision tree?
items.answer: CART (Classification And Regression Trees) recursively partitions training data with binary axis-aligned splits. At each node it searches feature thresholds, picks the split that maximizes impurity reduction (Gini or entropy for classification, variance for regression), and repeats on child nodes until stopping rules (max depth, min samples, pure leaf) apply.

items.question: Write the Gini impurity for a node with class proportions p₁, p₂, …, p_K.
items.answer: G = 1 − Σ_k p_k². For a pure node (one p_k = 1), G = 0. For binary classes with equal mix (p = 0.5 each), G = 0.5.

items.question: How is information gain computed for a candidate split?
items.answer: Gain = I(parent) − [(|L|/|S|)·I(L) + (|R|/|S|)·I(R)] where I is Gini or entropy, L and R are left/right child nodes, S is the parent. CART selects the split with maximum gain.

items.question: What prediction does a classification leaf and a regression leaf return?
items.answer: Classification leaf: majority class among training samples in that leaf (ties broken by convention in the library). Regression leaf: mean of the target y for samples in that leaf.

items.question: Why are single decision trees high-variance learners?
items.answer: Early splits are chosen greedily on finite data — small changes in training samples can change the best root split and reshape the entire tree. Deep trees amplify this by creating many data-dependent partitions.

items.question: Name three hyperparameters that control tree complexity and what each does.
items.answer: max_depth caps longest root-to-leaf path; min_samples_leaf requires at least that many samples per leaf; min_samples_split requires minimum samples to attempt a split. All reduce overfitting when increased (except max_depth, which reduces complexity when decreased).

items.question: Do decision trees require feature scaling? Why or why not?
items.answer: No. Splits are of the form feature j ≤ threshold t; monotonic rescaling of a single feature does not change the partition of samples — only the numeric threshold value changes.

items.question: When would Gini and entropy choose different splits?
items.answer: Rarely — they rank most candidate splits similarly. Differences appear at near-tie gains or when numerical precision matters. In practice, tune depth and leaf size first; criterion is a minor choice.
